candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Dan Wareing North West Google Police found a woman unresponsive at an address on Tweedale Street, in Rochdale A man has been arrested on suspicion of murder after a woman in her 40s was found dead Officers were called to an address on Tweedale Street, Rochdale, Greater Manchester at about 16:00 BST on Monday and the woman was pronounced dead at the scene A man his 30s was arrested on suspicion of murder and remains in custody for questioning, Greater Manchester Police said The force said it believed the incident was isolated and "posed no further risk to the wider public" "We appreciate this incident is likely to cause some distress amongst the public and local community," said Ch Supt Jamie Daniels "I would like to reassure you that we believe this to be an isolated incident, which does not pose any further risk to the wider public

candidates.title: 'We won't allow riots to happen again like in 2001'
candidates.summary: Communities in Oldham and Burnley are working together to ensure positive change continues
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review. | Validator: editorial_contract rejected item (story_type=planning, anchor=none, tier=filler).
candidates.practical_angle: Есть новый городской контекст для Greater Manchester.
candidates.lead: Communities in Oldham and Burnley are working together to ensure positive change continues
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Phil McCann North West PA Media Police officers in riot gear march past terraced housing in Oldham Just after the turn of the millennium – now a quarter of a century ago – the worst racial violence Britain had seen since the 1980s began in Ol It would spread to Burnley the following month, and cross the Pennines to hit Bradford in July Reports would later pin the blame partly on "deep seated" segregation, which authorities had "persistently failed" to face up to since the 1970s – leading to communities living what were termed "parallel lives" The government and councils were advised by Prof Ted Cantle to ensure schools were less segregated - or at least that they mixed more – and to use housebuilding programmes and regeneration as a way to stimulate neighbourhoods to be more ethnically diverse over time After the debris was cleaned up, local politicians, volunteers and community leaders spent the next 25 years trying to build bridges "This is a different Burnley from what we had 25 years ago, it's different communities, the challenges are different," said Afrasiab Anwar – who set up football classes for young people from both communities and went on to be a trustee of the cross-community group Building Bridge

candidates.title: Dark past of city's Green Quarter and the brave people who lived there
candidates.summary: Now known as the Green Quarter, Manchester's Angel Meadow was once described as "hell upon Earth"
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review. | Validator: editorial_contract rejected item (story_type=news, anchor=none, tier=filler).
candidates.practical_angle: Есть новый городской контекст для Greater Manchester.
candidates.lead: Now known as the Green Quarter, Manchester's Angel Meadow was once described as "hell upon Earth"
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Lynette Horsburgh North West Canon Grace Thomas Angel Meadow is now known as the Green Quarter It was described as "hell upon earth" and the "worst Victorian slum outside of London" Located just north east of Manchester city centre, Angel Meadow had formerly been an affluent, relatively rural area, until the Industrial Revolution hastily saw it transformed into a crime and disease-ridden slum Historian Dr Dean Kirby said people living in Angel Meadow, including some of his ancestors, "helped change the world and made Manchester what it is" but endured "horrific conditions", with half of the children living there dying before the age of five Now, the area's chequered past has inspired a new project looking at how it shaped the city's heritage led by Manchester Cathedral and more specifically Canon Grace Thomas who discovered more about its significant history on a parish walk Kirby, who wrote Angel Meadow: Victorian Britain's Most Savage Slum, said his ancestors, including his great, great, great grandfather, William Kirby, came to the area from Ireland to work and "make a new life for themselves" William was a fishmonger, and like many residents in Angel Meadow, was living underground in a cellar with his family which was not designed for humans to inhabit

"It's heartbreaking going through the hives and just coming across just piles of dead bees "A good strong colony will have 50,000 to 60,000 bees - going into winter they'll go down to a few thousand

candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Lynette Horsburgh North West PA Media Thousands of fans gathered for the open-top bus parade in the blistering heat Thousands of Manchester City fans have turned out to watch an emotional Blues' open-top bus parade to mark this season's succe Guardiola is leaving after 10 seasons in charge of the men's side, which has seen him lift 17 major trophies including the Premier League Cup and FA Cup Supporters also said farewell to legendary players Bernardo Silva and John Stones Avid city fans Josh and Carla said the celebrations for the silverware - which included the FA Cup, Carabao Cup, the Women's Super League trophy and the FA Youth Cup - was "bittersweet" as they were saying goodbye to Guardiola's glory days It was a sea of City scarves and flags throughout the parade route, which started at the Northern Quarter and finished at the Colin Bell Stand reception at the Etihad Stadium Meanwhile, more than 19,000 fans attended a separate event - The After Party - held at Co-op Live near the Etihad Stadium where players from the women's, men's and academy teams celebrated this season's successes

candidates.title: 'I photographed Bob Dylan as fan shouted Judas at Manchester gig'
candidates.summary: Photographer and fan Alan Corbett took shots of Dylan from the balcony at Manchester Free Trade Hall
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review. | Validator: editorial_contract rejected item (story_type=news, anchor=none, tier=filler).
candidates.practical_angle: Есть новый городской контекст для Greater Manchester.
candidates.lead: Photographer and fan Alan Corbett took shots of Dylan from the balcony at Manchester Free Trade Hall
candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Paul Burnell North West Alan Corbett The notorious heckle in Manchester is part of rock folklore Music fan Alan Corbett had no idea he would be snapping a piece of pop history when he took a camera to a Bob Dylan gig at Manchester Free Trade Corbett, then aged 19, had seen Dylan perform an acoustic set at the venue a year earlier on 7 May where he had seen his hero from the first two rows supported by the legendary Joan Baez The following year though, it was a different story in more ways than one For starters, Corbett was up on the balcony, but more memorably, in the second half of his set, Dylan had strapped a 1965 black Fender Telecaster electric guitar to himself "He was so popular, the only places we could get were in the balcony," said Corbett The fledgling photographer was still smarting from music newspaper Melody Maker rejecting his shots of jazz luminary Ornette Coleman when he took his camera into the gig

candidates.evidence_text: Share Save Add as preferred on Google Kaleigh Watterson Cheshire political reporter BBC, Labour Ben Fletcher and Dan Price have both been selected for the election next year The Conservative and Labour candidates standing to become mayor of Cheshire and Warrington have said they Elected mayors across England have the ability to raise a mayoral precept - a sum on top of council tax to raise additional funds for services - although most of them currently do not Conservative Ben Fletcher said "taxing people more was not the answer" and in response, Labour's Dan Price said he also had "no plans to introduce tax" The other major parties have not yet named their candidates The Cheshire and Warrington Combined Authority came into force earlier this year and the mayoral election will take place next year Businessman Fletcher said he would use his commercial experience to "lead an efficient and effective combined authority that creates plans that win private investment"

candidates.evidence_text: Image caption, Jenni Smith said the Loaves and Fishes charity is supporting on average about 160 new people every month Rising rent costs are pushing more people into rough sleeping and the new Renters Rights' Act needed to go further to support those in hardship, a charity has said The act came into force on 1 May and banned no-fault evictions, ended fixed-term tenancies and introduced new rules to stop bidding wars meaning renters cannot be asked to pay more than the advertised price Salford Loaves and Fishes, which supports homeless and vulnerable people in the city, said it was concerned it had not introduced caps on how much private landlords can increase rent The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) said it was "not looking at rent controls" as they could cause higher rents The latest government figures showed north-west England has seen a 20% increase in rough sleepers year-on-year , external , up from 367 in 2024 to 441 in 2025

Online agitators. Community fracture. For more than a year, the M.E.N. investigated how Oldham’s politics spi News Charlotte Hall Local Democracy Reporter 06:14, 26 May 2026 Everyone is screaming. The beige walls of the town hall chambers echo with it. Councillors talk over each other, jabbing accusatory fingers into the air, while residents in the public gallery pick up a sporadic chant This was the scene in November 2024, when an argument in the chambers became so heated the meeting had to be adjourned and riot police - who were on standby for a peaceful protest outside the town hall - were asked inside to ‘have a word’ Oldham regularly descends into chaos. Inside the town hall, meetings are frequently interrupted, councillors are accused of spying on each other, and elected representatives are exiled to abandoned parts of the building Protesters have been dragged out of the chambers. The council leader has had her car firebombed. Bullets carved with councillors’ names have allegedly turned up on their doorsteps But Oldham’s ‘toxic politics’ extends far beyond the walls of the Civic Centre. From Elon Musk pointing the global spotlight onto the rainy town in the North West of England, to being name dropped by the Prime Minister on subjects ranging from immigration to historic child sexual

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Makerfield by-election 2026 The heated opinions in the centre of political earthquake on a scorching Bank Holiday Monday The Manchester Evening News spoke to voters in Orrell about the upcoming Makerfield by-election Comments News Andrew Bardsley Cour It’s lunch time on Bank Holiday Monday and the streets of Orrell are almost deserted. And who could blame its residents? With the mercury tipping more than 30C on one of the hottest May days in living memory, it seems that not many people are venturing beyond their back gardens as they soak in the scorching sunshine Into this high temperature situation arrives your correspondent, notepad in hand and pen at the ready. Tasked with taking the political temperature in this leafy suburb ahead of next month’s hugely significant by-election, I spot the venue which seems to be receiving the highest Shoppers flock to the local store to pick up their last minute essentials for the day, a couple of bottles of wine or cans of beer to go with the barbecue, or a box of ice lollies to keep cool Politics may likely have not been an issue on their shopping lists on this record breaking bank holiday, but for those who spoke with the Manchester Evening News , it was certainly as issue which has engaged their mind and thoughts

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Courts On a squalid farm, they failed their son 'They ignored warnings, and at no point have they expressed responsibility or culpability for what happened' View 6 Images Daniel was just three years old when he lost his life News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 18:06, 25 May 2026 The parents of a little boy who was mauled to death by a huge dog have been told they could have 'easily prevented' the 'entirely avoidable' tragedy as they took their case to the Appeal Court. Joanne Bedford and Mark Twigg were ja The toddler was killed after he entered a pen where two guard dogs were being kept at Carr Farm in Milnrow , Rochdale . Twigg and Bedford were renting out the property from a family friend, Matthew Brown, who at the time was on remand in prison and was later jailed over a separat They had agreed to care for 11 dogs, including cane corso, American bulldog and a Boerboel breeds, being kept on the farm while he was away. Signs were in place warning visitors about the animals, including one which said ‘beware of the dogs they bite’ A jury at Manchester Crown Court heard the parents had been warned by RSPCA inspectors and social services about the dangers the dogs posed to Daniel. Their eldest son had also been bitten in the past The fatal injuries were believed to have been caused by a cane corso named Sid on May 15, 2022. He was shot dead by armed police

It can also have an impact on local wildlife and natural habitats Another issue is when a body of water is located on private land or is used for fishing. In a post on Facebook, the Peak District National Park explained the risks of wild swimming It stated: “Wild swimming causes issues with safety, private ownership, the impact on wildlife, and littering during busy seasons. Swimming is not permitted in any reservoir in the Peak District View 2 Images The Peak District is dotted with pools, reservoirs and rivers which can be tempting on hot days (Image: Getty Images)

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Manchester weather Hot weather health alert issued for the north west as temperatures soar There could 'significant impacts' on health and care services News Chris Slater Senior Reporter 12:54, 25 May 2026 View Image People soak up in Manchester city An alert has been issued by health bosses amid the soaring temperatures. The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) issues alerts which are designed to act as an early warning system for the health and social sectors in hot and cold weather And they are warning of possible 'significant impacts' in a yellow heat-health alert which has been issued for the North West. Temperatures could climb as high as 34 degrees celsius today (May 25) and 31C on tomorrow The UKHSA warns that there will be a 'likely increase in demand for health services' in the region during the heatwave. They said that internal temperatures in hospitals and care homes 'may exceed the recommended threshold for clinical risk assessment' and that the heat may affec It says there could be a 'rise in deaths, particularly among those aged 65 and over or with health conditions' and that there 'may also be impacts on younger age groups.' However it said the likelihood was 'low.' NHS North West said in a post on X: "Hot weather is forecast this bank holiday weekend. Whether you're staying at home or enjoying a family day out, please take extra care. Stay hydrated and wear sun protection."

Online agitators. Community fracture. For more than a year, the M.E.N. investigated how Oldham’s politics spi News Charlotte Hall Local Democracy Reporter 06:14, 26 May 2026 Everyone is screaming. The beige walls of the town hall chambers echo with it. Councillors talk over each other, jabbing accusatory fingers into the air, while residents in the public gallery pick up a sporadic chant This was the scene in November 2024, when an argument in the chambers became so heated the meeting had to be adjourned and riot police - who were on standby for a peaceful protest outside the town hall - were asked inside to ‘have a word’ Oldham regularly descends into chaos. Inside the town hall, meetings are frequently interrupted, councillors are accused of spying on each other, and elected representatives are exiled to abandoned parts of the building Protesters have been dragged out of the chambers. The council leader has had her car firebombed. Bullets carved with councillors’ names have allegedly turned up on their doorsteps But Oldham’s ‘toxic politics’ extends far beyond the walls of the Civic Centre. From Elon Musk pointing the global spotlight onto the rainy town in the North West of England, to being name dropped by the Prime Minister on subjects ranging from immigration to historic child sexual

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Makerfield by-election 2026 The heated opinions in the centre of political earthquake on a scorching Bank Holiday Monday The Manchester Evening News spoke to voters in Orrell about the upcoming Makerfield by-election Comments News Andrew Bardsley Cour It’s lunch time on Bank Holiday Monday and the streets of Orrell are almost deserted. And who could blame its residents? With the mercury tipping more than 30C on one of the hottest May days in living memory, it seems that not many people are venturing beyond their back gardens as they soak in the scorching sunshine Into this high temperature situation arrives your correspondent, notepad in hand and pen at the ready. Tasked with taking the political temperature in this leafy suburb ahead of next month’s hugely significant by-election, I spot the venue which seems to be receiving the highest Shoppers flock to the local store to pick up their last minute essentials for the day, a couple of bottles of wine or cans of beer to go with the barbecue, or a box of ice lollies to keep cool Politics may likely have not been an issue on their shopping lists on this record breaking bank holiday, but for those who spoke with the Manchester Evening News , it was certainly as issue which has engaged their mind and thoughts

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Peak District Warning issued to anyone planning on wild swimming in the Peak District as temperatures soar The message comes as temperatures soar across the UK News Liv Clarke Tourism writer 16:39, 25 May 2026 View 2 Images Reservoirs can be tempting The Peak District National Park has warned against people going wild swimming as temperatures soar across the UK this week While it can be tempting to cool off in a dip in the water outdoors, often ‘wild swimming’ locations can pose safety risks. It can also have an impact on local wildlife and natural habitats Another issue is when a body of water is located on private land or is used for fishing. In a post on Facebook, the Peak District National Park explained the risks of wild swimming It stated: “Wild swimming causes issues with safety, private ownership, the impact on wildlife, and littering during busy seasons. Swimming is not permitted in any reservoir in the Peak District View 2 Images The Peak District is dotted with pools, reservoirs and rivers which can be tempting on hot days (Image: Getty Images)

Campaigning continued for the much-anticipated by-election this weekend, after the Greater Manchester Mayor launched his campaign on Friday He told supporters British politics needed a 'new script' and that he was not offering 'more of the same'. Read our piece from the event here On Saturday evening, The Sunday Times published a poll showing a knife-edge race between Labour and Reform, whose candidate is local plumber, Robert Kenyon In the poll, based off just over 360 people, Mr Burnham had 43% of the vote, with Mr Kenyon receiving 40%. The next highest was the Restore Britain candidate, Rebecca Shepherd, with 7% On Sunday afternoon, tech billionaire Elon Musk - who has a history of endorsing right-wing parties in Europe - retweeted a video endorsing Rupert Lowe's Restore Britain Party

Online agitators. Community fracture. For more than a year, the M.E.N. investigated how Oldham’s politics spi News Charlotte Hall Local Democracy Reporter 06:14, 26 May 2026 Everyone is screaming. The beige walls of the town hall chambers echo with it. Councillors talk over each other, jabbing accusatory fingers into the air, while residents in the public gallery pick up a sporadic chant This was the scene in November 2024, when an argument in the chambers became so heated the meeting had to be adjourned and riot police - who were on standby for a peaceful protest outside the town hall - were asked inside to ‘have a word’ Oldham regularly descends into chaos. Inside the town hall, meetings are frequently interrupted, councillors are accused of spying on each other, and elected representatives are exiled to abandoned parts of the building Protesters have been dragged out of the chambers. The council leader has had her car firebombed. Bullets carved with councillors’ names have allegedly turned up on their doorsteps But Oldham’s ‘toxic politics’ extends far beyond the walls of the Civic Centre. From Elon Musk pointing the global spotlight onto the rainy town in the North West of England, to being name dropped by the Prime Minister on subjects ranging from immigration to historic child sexual

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Makerfield by-election 2026 The heated opinions in the centre of political earthquake on a scorching Bank Holiday Monday The Manchester Evening News spoke to voters in Orrell about the upcoming Makerfield by-election Comments News Andrew Bardsley Cour It’s lunch time on Bank Holiday Monday and the streets of Orrell are almost deserted. And who could blame its residents? With the mercury tipping more than 30C on one of the hottest May days in living memory, it seems that not many people are venturing beyond their back gardens as they soak in the scorching sunshine Into this high temperature situation arrives your correspondent, notepad in hand and pen at the ready. Tasked with taking the political temperature in this leafy suburb ahead of next month’s hugely significant by-election, I spot the venue which seems to be receiving the highest Shoppers flock to the local store to pick up their last minute essentials for the day, a couple of bottles of wine or cans of beer to go with the barbecue, or a box of ice lollies to keep cool Politics may likely have not been an issue on their shopping lists on this record breaking bank holiday, but for those who spoke with the Manchester Evening News , it was certainly as issue which has engaged their mind and thoughts

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Courts On a squalid farm, they failed their son 'They ignored warnings, and at no point have they expressed responsibility or culpability for what happened' View 6 Images Daniel was just three years old when he lost his life News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 18:06, 25 May 2026 The parents of a little boy who was mauled to death by a huge dog have been told they could have 'easily prevented' the 'entirely avoidable' tragedy as they took their case to the Appeal Court. Joanne Bedford and Mark Twigg were ja The toddler was killed after he entered a pen where two guard dogs were being kept at Carr Farm in Milnrow , Rochdale . Twigg and Bedford were renting out the property from a family friend, Matthew Brown, who at the time was on remand in prison and was later jailed over a separat They had agreed to care for 11 dogs, including cane corso, American bulldog and a Boerboel breeds, being kept on the farm while he was away. Signs were in place warning visitors about the animals, including one which said ‘beware of the dogs they bite’ A jury at Manchester Crown Court heard the parents had been warned by RSPCA inspectors and social services about the dangers the dogs posed to Daniel. Their eldest son had also been bitten in the past The fatal injuries were believed to have been caused by a cane corso named Sid on May 15, 2022. He was shot dead by armed police

The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) issues alerts which are designed to act as an early warning system for the health and social sectors in hot and cold weather And they are warning of possible 'significant impacts' in a yellow heat-health alert which has been issued for the North West. Temperatures could climb as high as 34 degrees celsius today (May 25) and 31C on tomorrow The UKHSA warns that there will be a 'likely increase in demand for health services' in the region during the heatwave. They said that internal temperatures in hospitals and care homes 'may exceed the recommended threshold for clinical risk assessment' and that the heat may affec It says there could be a 'rise in deaths, particularly among those aged 65 and over or with health conditions' and that there 'may also be impacts on younger age groups.' However it said the likelihood was 'low.' NHS North West said in a post on X: "Hot weather is forecast this bank holiday weekend. Whether you're staying at home or enjoying a family day out, please take extra care. Stay hydrated and wear sun protection."

The Met Office recorded 26C at its Rostherne weather station, near Altrincham , on Sunday (May 24) - while other parts of England saw conditions above 30C Those high temperatures are expected in Greater Manchester today (May 25), as the region is set for a bank holiday sizzler. A top temperature of 33C is forecast in Manchester, Salford , Stockport and Trafford - with clear, sunny skies and 'very high' UV levels, according to the M In Bolton , Bury , Oldham , Rochdale , Tameside and Wigan , the top temperature is expected to be an ever-so-slightly cooler 32C. Those balmy temperatures mean parts of Greater Manchester will be warmer than a host of popular European sunshine destinations today According to the Met Office forecasts, the whole region will see temperatures far exceeding the 24C expected in Limassol, Cyprus, or the 23C set for Istanbul, Turkey. Highs of 27C are forecast in Athens and Thessaloniki, Greece, and in Portugal's capital Lisbon Parts of Spain are also expected to be cooler than Greater Manchester, with Barcelona set for 26C and Malaga braced for 29C, according to the Met Office. candidates.lead: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules
candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Manchester Airport All the places you can't fly a drone in Greater Manchester and why Many drone operators could be flying under the radar without even realising it News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:15, 25 May 2026 View 3 Images There are strict rules At Strangeways prison drones are being used to deliver everything from drugs, mobile phones and even takeaways direct to inmates' cell windows. It's a glaring example of how criminals will find ways to exploit technology for crooked means But did you know it's actually illegal to fly a drone over the prison - and much of Manchester city centre - even if you aren't smuggling contraband? In fact there are several other no-fly zones across the region. And breaking those rules can be a serious crime Anyone caught flying in a restricted zone could face an unlimited fine and up to five years in prison. Drone expert Mark Leah, from Stockport-based Aztech Drones, says the strict rules are there to protect safety, security and privacy Mark, who's been a professional drone pilot for seven years, says all flyers should have a flyer ID and be aware of where they're allowed to fly and basic rules such as not flying above people or too close to buildings and keeping the drone in your line of sight at all times

candidates.lead: News Greater Manchester News Parenting My daughter was mocked for having Down syndrome, then a group of girls did something I'll never forget "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing
candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Parenting My daughter was mocked for having Down syndrome, then a group of girls did something I'll never forget "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing A mum has praised the 'heartwarming' gesture of a group of girls who stepped in to support her daughter when she was laughed at in the park. Vernice Jones had taken six-year-old Pixie Rose, who has Down syndrome, to play in the park after collecting her from school Knowing she gets 'stared at', Vernice said it was 'a massive worry' to take her somewhere different, but she wasn't expecting her daughter to be mocked by children just a few years older "Normally we go to the same park, which is local to our house, but I had a day off so I picked her up from school and went to a park a bit closer," said Vernice. "It's always a massive worry for me because I know that when people don't know her, she gets stared at "She's in a trolley because she struggles walking, or in a pram, but she's getting a little big for that now, and some girls were laughing and pointing and stuff at her." View 8 Images Pixie Rose Jones (Image: Manchester Family / MEN)

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Gay Village Attacked in the Gay Village, what happened next left him 'absolutely disgusted with police' Lewis Bell was brutally beaten as he was getting into a cab with his boyfriend, but says police waited too long to obtain CCTV of the incident View News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 06:14, 25 May 2026 Updated 06:25, 25 May 2026 A man who was brutally attacked at random while getting in a taxi in Manchester's Gay Village has hit out at the police after his case was closed, saying he feels 'absolutely disg Lewis Bell, 43, had been out with his fiancé Peter on April 4 and had visited bars in the village, including Via, The Goose and New York New York. The bank holiday celebrations then came to end when the pair decided to order a taxi home to Droylsden at around 10.30pm As Lewis was getting in the back of the car at the junction of Sackville Street and Bloom Street, he told the Manchester Evening News a man came up behind him and held the door open, before hurling homophobic slurs and repeatedly punching him When his partner then rushed out of the taxi to help, Lewis says a woman accompanying the offender held him back as Lewis was then struck to the throat and sent hurtling to the ground, causing him to suffer a severe head injury. He says the suspect then quickly fled the scene But around six weeks later, Lewis claimed he was informed by police that the case would be closed. He said he was told that it had been left too late to gather CCTV footage, which will have been overwritten, and that no further action could be taken to trace his attacker

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Football 'That third goal went in and I thought 'we’ve done it'- it brought me to tears' "It’s the fourth best day of my life behind meeting my partner and the births of my children" Comments News Amy Walker Court reporter and Charlotte Fisher Reporte Wembley Stadium was a cacophony of noise as Bolton Wanderers fans watched their beloved 'whites' into the Championship. Chants of 'We are going up' echoed around the stadium amid the huge roar of cheers It comes as Bolton Wanderers have been promoted to the Championship after a 4-1 win over Stockport County. Wanderers took the lead early through Ruben Rodrigues. Adama Sidibeh had an equaliser ruled out for a foul by VAR but equalised on 30 minutes After the break, Bolton were the better side and took the lead when Kyle Wootton turned the ball into his own net. Sam Dalby made it three with a wonderful overhead kick to seal the result Stockport had Josh Dacres-Cogley sent off late for a hair pull on Ibrahim Cissoko. Rodrigues scored the penalty to seal the win Despite the sweltering weather , fans from both rival Greater Manchester sides made the big trip to Wembley for the play-off final in the hopes that they would see their team through to promotion

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Altrincham Erling Haaland has been her inspiration, but the NHS 'won’t give Phoebe that chance' Phoebe's only chance of long term survival is a liver transplant - but the NHS will not do it View 7 Images Erling Haaland has sent a message of encouragem Comments News Neal Keeling Chief reporter 17:29, 24 May 2026 Updated 17:33, 24 May 2026 He has been her inspiration. Now Erling Haaland has sent a heartfelt message to a little girl whose zest for life has not diminished despite needing a liver transplant which the NHS will not c Until last autumn Phoebe Clarke did martial arts, swimming, basketball, and rugby. And driven by watching Manchester City's number nine, she also played football for Broadheath Central under 9s, her local team in Altrincham Phoebe has a rare liver cancer called hepatic angiosarcoma which was diagnosed in February last year. Particularly aggressive, it can only be fully treated by surgically removing the affected areas; in Phoebe's case every part of her remaining liver is cancerous. She has had four Her only chance of long term survival is a liver transplant. But the NHS will not do it as the chances of her condition returning are considered too high View 7 Images Phoebe Clarke who is living with a rare form of liver cancer

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News True crime series The New York gangster who vowed to 'christen Strangeways in blood' - then made friends with Charles Bronson Clifton 'Yankee' Jeter is one of the UK's most dangerous prisoners View 5 Images Clifton 'Yankee' Jeter News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 15:52, 24 May 2026 Updated 16:07, 24 May 2026 The preparations were as precise as they were chilling. First Clifton 'Yankee' Jeter fashioned a deadly weapon by embedding a razor blade in the melted handle of a plastic knife Next the former New York gangster greased up his arms with baby oil to make restraint more difficult. Only then did he launch the brutal attack that left two Strangeways prison guards fighting for their life - and Jeter facing up to the prospect of spending the rest of his days b British-born Jeter moved to the US as a child but was deported back to the UK after getting involved with some of New York's most notorious street gangs In 2002, he was jailed for seven years in the UK for aggravated burglary after raiding a hotel room and holding a man at gunpoint. Just five years later he was arrested again after a 'honey trap' robbery went wrong, resulting in the murder of dad-of-seven Ghiass Tarabin in Hendon A trial at the Old Bailey heard the gangster's girlfriend Candy had unexpectedly turned up at the victim's home for a sexual liaison. But it was just a ruse. Jeter burst in demanding cash and valuables

The East Lancashire Railway says it has again been the victim of a 'deliberate' and 'disgraceful' act of vand Police are investigating after a locomotive and several coaches from the Inter City 125 heritage set were targeted in what the railway describe as a mindless attack, with extensive graffiti across the rolling stock In a statement the East Lancashire railway said "This was not opportunistic behaviour — it was a calculated act of destruction against irreplaceable heritage assets cared for by volunteers "Once discovered, volunteers immediately stepped in and worked tirelessly for several hours using specialist cleaning materials to remove the graffiti before it could permanently set "Their swift action prevented what could have been significant and lasting damage, although further polishing and restoration work is still required."

candidates.evidence_text: Tuesday 21 April 2026 at 4:58pm Drivers at Greater Manchester Mental Health Trust will begin three further weeks of strikes. Credit: Jordan Pettitt/PA Mental health staff are set to strike over pay, UNISON has confirmed Drivers at Greater Manchester Mental Health Trust will begin three further weeks of strikes after bosses ignored their own report and failed to improve staff pay, the union reports The drivers - who transport samples, medication, linen and food across Greater Manchester – say they have already completed a job evaluation that found their roles should be upgraded to a higher pay grade But the union says bosses have not acted on the findings and have instead asked for a second opinion from another NHS trust. It says 11 drivers have been left thousands of pounds out of pocket UNISON North West regional organiser Paddy Cleary said: "Trust bosses have failed to act in time and workers have been left with no choice but to strike. Nobody wants to take this step, but staff are underpaid for the vital work they do – as the trust’s own report makes clear "The job evaluation process was followed correctly. These staff should be on a higher rate of pay. Instead of putting this right, senior managers have chosen to delay the inevitable by seeking unnecessary advice

Plans for the proje Trafford Council’s planning committee debated the scheme for an hour yesterday evening before deciding to reject the plans In recommending the project for approval, officers had identified a series of drawbacks but said the pros – job creation, a projected annual £100m GVA boost to the local economy, and a £12m contribution towards the creation of the £130m Carrington Relief Road – outweighed the con However, it became clear early on in the meeting that members of the planning committee felt the scheme would do more harm than good

candidates.lead: Iceni is leading the advisory team.
candidates.evidence_text: Iceni is leading the advisory team. Credit: planning documents A report has been lodged with the council seeking to clear the way for up to 390 homes off Starling Road in Ainsworth by the housebuilder The scheme is billed as Pilkington’s Fold, the name of the farmstead at the centre of the site. Taylor Wimpey’s project team is led by Iceni Projects, and also includes i-Transport and CSA Environmental The site is described by Iceni as being at the edge of the suburban area, close to the settlements of Radcliffe and Starling. It is a mostly rectangular site, narrowing to the north, with its southern boundary running up to the back gardens of houses fronting the Bolton-Bury Road Originally used for farming in the 18th century, the farmstead at the centre of the site is on Bury’s draft local list, potentially presenting a stumbling block In this non-designated heritage asset assessment, the development team is seeking to demonstrate that while a historic farmstead once stood at the heart of its proposed site, the buildings there now are more modern and don’t demonstrate any value meriting inclusion in the list

candidates.evidence_text: People across the North West are benefiting from life-saving lung cancer screening, with new figures showing that 1,700 people across Greater Manchester have now been diagnosed through the NHS Lung Cancer Screening Programme The milestone comes as the NHS announces that more than 10,000 people nationwide have now been diagnosed with lung cancer through screening — helping detect the disease earlier, when treatment is more likely to be successful The targeted programme invites people aged 55 to 74 who currently smoke or have smoked in the past for a lung health check. Those identified as high risk are then offered a low-dose CT scan, capable of detecting lung cancer before symptoms develop In Greater Manchester, NHS Lung Cancer Screening is jointly organised by the Greater Manchester Cancer Alliance and Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust with support from the Northern Care Alliance NHS Foundation Trust. Almost 80% of cancers being diagnosed at an earlier st One person who benefitted from an earlier cancer diagnosis through the NHS Lung Cancer Screening Programme is Ken Roberts, a 74 year-old manufacturing company director from Ladybridge, Bolton Ken was invited for a lung health check when a mobile scanning unit was parked at Morrisons in Bolton

Burnham’s victory in the 2021 mayoral election was widely predicted, but the scale of it was staggering: he had won not just in every borough o Our call wasn’t supposed to be about Burnham, per se. I was writing a profile for The Mill about Sir Richard Leese, the outgoing leader of Manchester City Council, who was stepping down after 25 years running the city, and Burnham had agreed to contribute his thoughts. I wondered “We’ve had our moments,” Burnham said, with what we both knew to be considerable understatement. After Burnham became mayor, he had asked Leese to be his deputy, but the region’s two most influential figures were odd bedfellows, and they regularly clashed “I am different in style, I guess,” Burnham said, when I asked him to explain what the difference was. Burnham admitted that as his first mayoral term wore on, Leese would accuse him of bringing in his “ Westminster ways ,” — like announcing things without getting proper sign off Who is Andy Burnham and what kind of prime minister would he be? candidates.evidence_text: The Mill investigates a series of mudslinging anonymous email accounts ahead of next week's local elections Hey! Jack Dulhanty here , staff writer at The Mill. Our local election coverage aims to take readers deeper into the contests for council seats across Greater Manchester, focussing on key characters who are having big impacts on the city region’s politics. Today’s piece is on a f In late February, not long after the death of England’s longest serving councillor, David Lancaster, Salford’s Labour councillors received an email. It was to confirm that a by-election would be held for Lancaster’s seat — Barton and Winton — barely two weeks before the local ele This was unusual. When a seat is vacated so close to a local election, normal practice is to put it to the polls alongside the other seats set to be contested. But, if at least two members of an opposition party call for it, the council is compelled to put on a by-election. This Sign up for The Mill Manchester's quality newspaper, delivered via email “In all my years it has never happened,” says Barbara Bentham, a councillor in Salford who worked closely with Lancaster for years. “We usually wait until after the funeral.” Indeed, Lancaster’s family had hardly begun arranging his funeral when the by-election was called. candidates.evidence_text: Share on WhatsApp Share via Email A 56-year-old man has been charged with kidnap and a series of sexual offences following the abduction of a teenage girl in Altrincham earlier this week Paul Sharpe, 55, from Stockport, has been charged with kidnap with intent to commit a sexual offence, sexual assault, attempted sexual assault, engaging in sexual activity in the presence of a child, threats with a bladed article, and administering a substance with intent Sharpe has been remanded in custody and is due to appear at Manchester Magistrates’ Court today The charges follow the abduction of a 15-year-old girl, who was snatched from a street in Altrincham at around 6.45pm on Monday She was found safe in the early hours of Tuesday morning in the car park of the Cottons Hotel and Spa in Knutsford Detective Chief Inspector Justin Bryant, of the Criminal Investigation Department in Trafford, said: “Officers are continuing to work tirelessly on this investigation to establish the full circumstances surrounding the incident

candidates.summary: The college was permanently established in Hale Barns in 1946
candidates.reason: Curator drop: Evergreen-листинг без срочного новостного повода — юбилей школы с мероприятиями в 2027 году, нет влияния на жителей GM сегодня.

candidates.lead: The college was permanently established in Hale Barns in 1946
candidates.evidence_text: The college was permanently established in Hale Barns in 1946 Share on WhatsApp Share via Email An aerial view of St Ambrose College today St Ambrose College is to celebrate its 80th anniversary with a year-long programme of events - with tickets for the centrepiece gala dinner already on sale and selling fast The dinner takes place at The Midland Hotel on Saturday 20th March 2027, hosted by Mike Toolan, and is open to everyone with a connection to the school, past or present. Tickets are available at www.stambroseball.co.uk A working party of governors, trustees, staff, parents and the Sports Committee is already planning a wider programme of events, which will also include concerts, a history wall, the launch of a Memorial Garden and a dedicated programme for Old Ambrosians The college was permanently established by the Christian Brothers at Woodeaves, Hale Barns in 1946, following the wartime evacuation of Les Vauxbelets College, Guernsey in 1940. Over eight decades it has served Catholic communities across Cheshire and Greater Manchester. With man Andy Aldrich, Chair of the Parents' Association SAPA, said: "Although I'm new to the St Ambrose community this year, it has already become clear why the school holds such a special place in the hearts of so many families and former students

Officers from Greater Manchester Police and Cheshire Police located the vehicle in the car park of the Cottons Hotel and Spa in Knutsford, where the teenager was found inside A statement from Greater Manchester Police said: "Working alongside Cheshire Police, we have arrested a man on suspicion of abduction after a teenage girl was reported missing from Altrincham on Monday 18 May "A 56‑year‑old man was arrested in the early hours of 19 May on Manchester Road, Knutsford, and remains in custody for further questioning

candidates.lead: Share on WhatsApp Share via Email A disused pub and motel in West Timperley have been boarded up and fenced off after a series of fires and trespassers gained entry to the long-empty buildings The former Pelican Inn and Altrincham North Lodge on Manchester Road have stood empty s
candidates.evidence_text: Share on WhatsApp Share via Email A disused pub and motel in West Timperley have been boarded up and fenced off after a series of fires and trespassers gained entry to the long-empty buildings The former Pelican Inn and Altrincham North Lodge on Manchester Road have stood empty since 2020, but Trafford Council has now served a notice on the site's owner - Suffolk-based brewery Greene King - under the Building Act 1984 That signals its intention to carry out emergency measures, including possible demolition, to make the buildings safe. Greene King is expected to cover the cost of the work Most recently, five fire engines were called to the site in April after a blaze broke out on the first floor of the former hotel, the second such fire in three years The Old Pelican Inn was designed by renowned local architect George Wescott and the buildings have been fenced off pending expected redevelopment of the site Cllr Rose Thompson, Trafford Council's Executive Member for Communities and Safety, said the council was pleased the buildings had been secured

candidates.evidence_text: They were donated to to Friends of Parks groups across Trafford Share on WhatsApp Share via Email Outgoing Mayor of Trafford Cllr Jane Brophy has marked the end of her mayoral year with a colourful gift to the borough's parks and green spaces Cllr Brophy donated around 100 hydrangea plants, purchased from Timperley's own Chris Walker Plants, to Friends of Parks groups across Trafford The plants were handed out over the bank holiday weekend across five hubs covering all corners of the borough, with the John Leigh Park Hub serving as the local distribution point for Altrincham and surrounding areas Parks and green spaces in the hub to receive plants included John Leigh Park, Stamford Park, Halecroft Park, Dunham Park, Navigation Road Park, Woodheys Park, Newton Park, Woodstock Park, Pickering Lodge, Timperley Green, De-Quincey Park, Denzell Gardens and Moss Park Gardens Local civic societies - including Altrincham in Bloom, Hale Civic Society, Timperley Civic Society, Altrincham Civic Society and Friends of Moss Park Gardens - also received plants

candidates.evidence_text: Share on WhatsApp Share via Email Households across Altrincham have just days left to renew their garden waste subscription - or risk gaps in their collections Trafford Council is urging residents to sign up before May 15th to guarantee they receive their new lilac 2026/27 permit in time for the new subscription year, which begins on June 1st The current yellow 2025/26 permits will no longer be valid after May 31st, meaning any bin without a lilac sticker on display will be skipped by collection crews Cllr Stephen Adshead, Trafford Council's Executive Member for Highways, Environmental and Traded Services, said: "Spring has well and truly arrived so there’s going to be more garden waste that needs collecting. Our collection teams will only collect garden waste from bins with a The council is also attaching reminder tags to bins across the borough The annual subscription price is unchanged from last year: £45 online or £50 by phone. candidates.evidence_text: You may have heard that Greater Manchester is introducing a new way of deciding which children and young people receive an NHS autism or ADHD assessment, and how quickly they are seen. We know this may raise questions or concerns for families In this article, we’re sharing clear and simple information about what these changes mean in Stockport, and where you can get advice and support. Some local details are still being finalised, and we’ll add updates as soon as they are confirmed. For more information, you can also We understand how hard it can be waiting for an assessment, and how difficult it can be to know where to turn for help in the meantime. That’s why we’ve introduced a new way of getting early support from the moment you first notice signs that your child or young person may be aut We know this is a lot to take in. If you’d like to find out more, ask questions, or talk things through, you’re very welcome to come along to one of our information drop-in sessions The Strategic Lead for Neurodivergence in Stockport will be attending the following drop-in sessions supported by the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS), Parents and Carers Together Stockport (PACTStockport), and Stockport Parents of ADHD Children in Education (SP In-person session with CAMHS and PACTStockport on Monday 11th May 10.30am-12pm, at Alma Lodge, 149 Buxton Road, Stockport, SK2 6EL. candidates.evidence_text: The historic lights on local landmark, Queen’s Park Bridge, are blazing once again, with final touches following its multi-million pound restoration now complete The bridge was recently partially rebuilt and restored by Heywood company, BDB Special Projects Limited, who used original drawings to carefully recreate the structure, so it has the same look and feel as it did when it was first unveiled in the 1930s As part of the project, specialists from Middleton company, Metcraft, were brought in to restore the beautiful gold and green lighting columns, which had once stood on the bridge. Although the lighting columns were beyond repair, Metcraft were able to salvage enough of the origin The lights were then reinstalled on the bridge and now have an electricity supply, enabling them to light up each evening from dusk to complement the existing street lights and highlight the bridge’s many heritage features The lights are energy efficient and can also be lit in different colours to honour special days in the calendar Councillor Shah Wazir, cabinet member for highways, said

candidates.title: Health column: Smoke Free Generation - A rare landmark moment for public health
candidates.summary: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tob
candidates.reason: Collected from live source; pending dedupe review.

candidates.lead: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tob
candidates.evidence_text: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tobacco to anyone born on, or after, 1st January 2009 - and, in doing so, creating the UK’s first ‘smoke‑free generation’ Make no mistake that this is one of the most significant public health reforms in a generation, and one that sends a clear signal: preventing harm before it begins matters Smoking remains the leading preventable cause of illness and early death in the UK. It significantly increases a person’s risk of cancer, heart disease and stroke, places huge pressure on the NHS, and continues to widen the gap in life expectancy across our communities The new law takes a bold, long‑term approach to breaking that cycle by ensuring today’s children never legally start smoking at all We know that information, advice and individual willpower can only go so far. Real, lasting change happens when policy and environments support people to make healthier choices

candidates.lead: A move towards rounded‑tip knives is underway across Wigan Borough to enhance community safety Through its Community Safety Partnership, Wigan Council is among the first local authority to take part in the safer knife replacement scheme, a key part of the Let’s Be Blunt campaign
candidates.evidence_text: A move towards rounded‑tip knives is underway across Wigan Borough to enhance community safety Through its Community Safety Partnership, Wigan Council is among the first local authority to take part in the safer knife replacement scheme, a key part of the Let’s Be Blunt campaign - set up by Leanne Lucas, a survivor of the Southport attack Nearly 20 council and partner buildings have already made the switch to the knives which feature a rounded-ended tip, as well as many residential homes taking up the pledge and making the swap Leanne Lucas said: “Replacing pointed tip knives is a simple but important step in reducing risk and preventing harm “Rounded tips make a real difference; the pointed tip creates the pressure point and increases the potential for serious injury “We also must recognise that harm is often about access and opportunity. When everyday items are easily available, they can be used in moments of crisis, impulse or vulnerability, so making small changes like this helps reduce that immediate risk

candidates.evidence_text: Venture Arts artist Sally Hirst is currently an Artist in Residence at the gallery where her practice has been influenced through the Whitworth’s exhibitions, collections, programme, and visitors Venture Arts artist Sally Hirst is currently an Artist in Residence in our Everyday Art School gallery where her practice is influenced by the Whitworth’s exhibitions, collections, programmes, and visitors Sally is a socially engaged illustrator, textile artist and ceramicist with much of her work being inspired by the people around her Sally is particularly interested in social history and retelling the stories of ordinary people from the past. These have included Holocaust survivors, ‘witches’ and Victorian factory workers. She likes to link these stories to her own experiences, such as fostering refugees and Accessibility is at the heart of everything Sally creates. Such as writing audio descriptions in the form of poetry, writing easy reads, and delivering neurodivergent friendly tours You can find Sally most Thursdays in our Everyday Art School space on our Mezzanine

candidates.lead: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović .
candidates.evidence_text: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović . Regarded as the world’s most influential performance artist, Abramović’s transformative work has reached millions of people – from opening the Pyramid Stage at Glastonbury Festival t A four-hour ritual where ancient myth meets performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic explores the eroticism, spirituality and traditions of Abramović’s homeland through 13 visceral scenes Brought to life by a cast of over 70 performers, dancers, musicians and singers, the experience unfolds across Aviva Studios. Audiences are free to choose their own path – with pop-up encounters punctuating the action, bringing bodies together in dance, song and ritual Watch Fertility Rite , a fevered ritual where bodies writhe against the ground in a desperate call for fertility. Witness Massaging the Breast where women gesticulate over graves to awaken the earth. In Scaring the Gods , women bare themselves to the sky to banish storms. Listen Unafraid to push the boundaries of eroticism and performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic asks its audience a timeless question: What are our bodies truly for?

Drawing inspiration from the Voyager deep-space probes launched in 1977 which dedicated a lifetime to venturing towards the unknown, Voyage attempts to pause the present moment and lead us into an uncharted Inspired by the repetitive actions of the traditional Japanese cup-and-ball toy ‘kendama’, Ryu Suzuki ’s Hakkō explores the gradual transformation of body and mind through focussed iterations of the simplest movements. The dancers enter a trance-like state of deep concentration, Korea’s only national contemporary dance company, KNCDC collaborates with artists of outstanding creative vision to create contemporary dance works that reflect history, society, and everyday life in modern Korea—resonating with audiences across regions and generations. In May 20 Young-doo Jung is a choreographer, director, dancer and actor who experiments with a wide range of forms and systems in choreography and composition, focusing on margins, breaths and subtle changes rather than excessive movement. Wed 27 May 2026 7:30PM Theatre Music Wed 27 May 2026 7:30PM Lyric Theatre Band A Standard £22.00 Access £22.00 Band B Standard £19.00 Access £19.00 Band C Standard £16.00 Access £16.00 Restricted View Restricted View £19.00 Restricted View 2nd Price After a sensational 2025 that saw over 60,000 Tina Turner fans across the UK singing, dancing, and having the time of their lives, What’s Love Got To Do With It? is back and bigger than ever for 2026! This electrifying tribute show celebrates the music and unparalleled career of Get ready for an unforgettable evening of high-energy, feel-good rock and soul, brought to life by a phenomenal live band. Packed with all the classic hits you know and love, the show delivers showstopping performances of Proud Mary, River Deep – Mountain High, Simply The Best, P Stepping into Tina’s iconic heels is Kent’s own Holly Bannis , the daughter of Greg Bannis , former lead singer of Hot Chocolate . Holly’s rise to center stage is as inspiring as it is exciting. Growing up in a musical family, Holly honed her craft for over a decade performing se “I had to audition as three of my musical icons, and Tina was an obvious choice for me. During lockdown, I studied her performances obsessively, getting into her moves, her walk, and her energy.” It wasn’t long before the team behind What’s Love Got To Do With It? noticed Holly’s raw talent and passion. Initially cast as a backing singer and understudy, Holly quickly stepped into the spotlight, making the lead role her own and earning standing ovations night after night

candidates.evidence_text: PHM’s headline exhibition for 2026, On The Line: 100 years of strikes & solidarity (on show until 2 November 2026), is accompanied by an immersive programme of events exploring the exhibition’s themes and the stories represented in PHM’s nationally recognised collection Find out more about the exhibition and the full programme of events below Step into our new Family Friendly Strike Den where you can dress up, speak up, read stories, and create protest art, inspired by strikes and solidarity explored throughout the museum Explore strikes and solidarity and create textiles inspired by your discussions in this artist led workshop You are invited to power up your imagination and build your own mini robot from a simple sheet of paper! Inspired by the Deceptioncon robot costume currently on display in On The Line , which once stood on the picket line in support of workers’ rights Explore a century of struggles and stories of strength drawing on the museum’s nationally significant collections. This free exhibition is part of the national commemorations, marking 100 years since the 1926 General Strike

candidates.lead: The Manchester Metropolitan University (MMU) Human Sciences Seminar Series for 2026, Philosophies of Resistance, will explore the philosophical thought that has resisted colonial, racial and carceral oppression The 2026 series aims is to question the role that philosophical ideas
candidates.evidence_text: The Manchester Metropolitan University (MMU) Human Sciences Seminar Series for 2026, Philosophies of Resistance, will explore the philosophical thought that has resisted colonial, racial and carceral oppression The 2026 series aims is to question the role that philosophical ideas have played – and continue to play – in collective struggles for social justice, and human emancipation more broadly Frantz Fanon & Stand Up to Racism, Wed 25 March 2026 Each seminar includes an academic talk followed by an ‘in-conversation’ discussion between the speaker(s) and a local political activist, and questions from the audience The MMU Human Sciences Seminar Series is an invited speaker and research seminar organised by the Philosophy section of MMU’s Department of History, Politics and Philosophy The series, which has run for over forty years, was founded by Philosopher David Melling and Professor Wolfe Mays in 1979. It was created out of a desire to explore the various human sciences in a systematic way from the standpoint of critical philosophy

candidates.evidence_text: A Lovely Weekend comedy festival Fairfield Social Club, Irk St, Manchester M4 4JT Fri 29th May, 2026 - Sun 31st May, 2026 A Lovely Weekend is back this spring, taking over Fairfield Social Club for three days of stand-up, live podcasts and some of the sharpest names on the UK comedy circuit Running from Friday 29 May to Sunday 31 May 2026, the festival packs around 20 shows into one weekend, bringing together established favourites, fast-rising names and cult comics for what’s shaping up to be one of the most stacked comedy bills in the city Friday kicks things off with Harriet Kemsley, fresh from winning over a whole new audience on Last One Laughing, alongside viral stand-up Tom Lawrinson, whose offbeat delivery and internet-born chaos have made him one of the most recognisable new voices in British comedy Saturday sees the return of Mark Silcox, who sold out at the festival last year and remains one of the strangest and funniest acts around, plus the first ever live edition of Sean McLoughlin’s podcast The Sean McTalk Show Then on Sunday, Sean McLoughlin is joined by stand-up favourite Josh Pugh, newly announced SNL UK cast member Larry Dean and acclaimed French comedian and writer Celya AB, rounding off the weekend with a line-up that feels properly loaded from start to finish

candidates.evidence_text: No Art Open Air at Bolesworth Castle Bolesworth Castle, Tattenhall, Chester CH3 9HQ Sat 30th May, 2026 12pm - 11pm No Art is heading out of the city and into the grounds of Bolesworth Castle – not far from Chester – for what’s set to be the party brand’s biggest UK open-air show to date Taking place on Saturday 30 May, the one-day festival has been curated by Dutch duo ANOTR and marks the first time a No Art open-air event outside the Netherlands has expanded into a full two-stage format. That means a bigger site, more room for large-scale production and install ANOTR will headline the day themselves, joined by Marco Carola, Max Dean, Toman, Benja and Silvie Loto on the main stage, all artists who fit neatly into No Art’s polished, forward-thinking house world. Over on the second stage, things lean deeper and looser, with Chaos In The CB Set across 6,000 acres, the Cheshire castle site gives the event a lot more room to breathe than your standard field set-up. Organisers are promising upgraded sound, ambitious production and immersive art installations, with the countryside location also helping deliver a cleaner No Art has built its name by pairing house music with striking settings and a strong visual identity, with previous editions landing in Amsterdam, Barcelona, Miami, Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo. This one looks like its most ambitious move in the UK so far, and easily its biggest

candidates.summary: Open Fire Dining Club: The Royal Oak x Tartuffe Torr Vale Mill, Torrvale Rd, High Peak, SK22 3HS Sat 30th May, 2026 - Sun 31st May, 2026 4pm - 9:30pm The Royal Oak was the name that kept coming up on a recent trip to Cartmel. So we went

candidates.lead: Open Fire Dining Club: The Royal Oak x Tartuffe Torr Vale Mill, Torrvale Rd, High Peak, SK22 3HS Sat 30th May, 2026 - Sun 31st May, 2026 4pm - 9:30pm The Royal Oak was the name that kept coming up on a recent trip to Cartmel.
candidates.evidence_text: Open Fire Dining Club: The Royal Oak x Tartuffe Torr Vale Mill, Torrvale Rd, High Peak, SK22 3HS Sat 30th May, 2026 - Sun 31st May, 2026 4pm - 9:30pm The Royal Oak was the name that kept coming up on a recent trip to Cartmel. So we went. And it more than lived up to the chatter Run by Demi Millard and Tom Pemberton, it’s a proper village pub with a very clear sense of place – rooted in the produce, landscape and flavours of the Lake District, but with cooking that goes far beyond what you might expect from a country inn. It felt like a natural fit for O On Saturday 30 and Sunday 31 May , The Royal Oak will head to Torr Vale Mill to collaborate with Tartuffe on an eight-course menu built around the Lakes , and the ingredients that define that part of the world The menu opens with Carlingford oysters, grilled over the Big Green Egg with wild garlic and crème fraîche, followed by a smoked chicken wing with Morecambe Bay brown shrimp – flavours that will immediately place you in the South Lakes At the centre of the evening is Hebridean lamb à la ficelle , served with white beans, anchoïade, courgette, dandelion, bitter leaf salad and The Royal Oak’s mint sauce. It’s exactly the kind of generous, elemental dish Open Fire Dining Club was made for

candidates.evidence_text: Yan Tan Tethera Farmer’s Market at Track Track Brewing Co - Brewery & Taproom, Unit 18, Piccadilly Trading Estate, Manchester M1 2NP Sun 31st May, 2026 12pm - 8pm Yan Tan Tethera Farmer’s Market is back for its May edition, and this time it’s turning the dial up a bit On Sunday 31 May, the market returns as a full Food Festival edition, bringing together farmers, growers, chefs, drinks, music and a very strong line-up of Northern kitchens for a one-day celebration of the start of summer Already confirmed are Cafe San Juan, Erst, Freds, Stockport Provisions, Track Beer and Slice Culture Pizza, with fresh oysters, street food and more names still to be announced In proper Yan Tan Tethera style, each food vendor will be paired with one of the market’s produce stalls to create special one-off dishes built around what’s in season. Think chefs and growers side by side, swapping ingredients, cooking over fire, and leaning into a bit of creati The market takes its name from the old Northern shepherd’s counting system, a nod to rural life, seasonal rhythms and the people who work the land. What started as a small, thoughtful farmers’ market has grown into a proper meeting point for farmers, cooks, artists and the local

candidates.evidence_text: After captivating the nation’s heart as the winner of Britain’s Got Talent 2017, and announcing the imminent release of his debut album ‘Our Generation’ on Syco Music November 17th, multi-instrumentalist, composer and producer, Tokio Myers recently announced a show at The Albert Tokio Myers is totally unique. He’s created a sound all his own, fusing an array of musical influences that have shaped him as an artist, ranging from classical and cinematic to hip-hop, dance and 90s’ soul Tokio grew up in North London before being awarded a coveted piano scholarship at the Royal College of Music. A stint as a studio musician led to recording and touring with some of the biggest names in music including Amy Winehouse, The Police, Kanye West and Mr Hudson. Now those Tokio has recently released the beautiful single ‘Angel’ from his hotly anticipated album ‘Our Generation’ and it is absolutely gorgeous!

candidates.evidence_text: Nathaniel Rateliff is a striking, emotive tenor vocalist and songwriter whose work runs the gamut from simple folk and Americana to roots rock & roll to vintage rhythm & blues. He and his band the Night Sweats are based in Denver, Colorado. After fronting two earlier bands, Ratel Rateliff had begun writing songs that required a band, so he formed the Night Sweats. Their rowdy retro sound was deeply influenced by vintage soul, R&B and gospel traditions, Van Morrison and the Band. A formidable live unit with a reputation for stage-quaking shows, they signed Nathaniel Rateliff & the Night Sweats’ self-titled debut album was issued in August 2015, preceded by appearances at the Newport Folk Festival and The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon. They toured globally in support of the album. While still on the road, they issued A Little S Be sure to catch Nathaniel Ratecliff and The Night Sweats live at Albert Hall on the 14th April 2018!

Each performance is a vibrant expression of spiritual passion, featuring the The atmosphere includes whirling Sufi dancing and a communal, ecstatic energy that transcends language or belief. Though born in the Sufi Islamic tradition, this music invites all people–regardless of background-into a shared space of unity and divine love. candidates.evidence_text: Over within the grounds of one of Levenshulme’s most popular parks is a community cafe that’s doing things a little differently Starting off life as a little coffee trike back in 2021, and now based inside a bright-yellow shipping container in Levenshulme ‘s Cringle Park, Grounded MCR CIC is a social enterprise that does more than just make you a brew Set up with the idea of creating a welcoming space for the whole community to gather, with a key focus on sustainability, Grounded serves up specialty coffee, lots of other hot and cold drinks, and yummy homemade food – much of which is grown by hand, on and around the container Coffee beans sourced from a local roaster that pays farmers properly, tasty bakes on offer from a handful of local makers – including bread from the incredible Stockport-based charity, Pure Innovations – and dozens of free community events organised throughout the year are just a Sustainability – and affordability, especially amid the ongoing cost of living crisis – are two huge driving factors for Grounded, especially through their partnership with FareShare Greater Manchester and utilising the ‘Too Good To Go’ scheme By delivering and using surplus food that would’ve otherwise gone to waste, Grounded is able to keep their prices low whilst still creating some delicious scran

candidates.evidence_text: A tiny Italian cafe that started life in a shipping container has opened its very own cafe and deli in the Northern Quarter Marleo, one of the most authentic Italian spots in Manchester, has transformed a unit on Oldham Street into a sunny yellow Sicilian cafe Here, you’ll find a counter stuffed with classic Sicilian street food, like perfect cone-shaped arancini, fresh focaccia sandwiches, and pizzette Maria and Leo are the couple behind this Northern Quarter newcomer (though you might already know them from Pollard Yard ), and they want to create a space that echoes the magic of the Italian island You can order a coffee to drink at the counter, the true Italian way, with a cannoli or a cornetto pastry on the side There are vibrant salads to take away, along with all those doughy Italian delights to go

candidates.evidence_text: The £35 million project set within 154 acres has transformed the former site of Worsley New Hall, restoring the historic walled garden, lake and surviving buildings Originally built for the 1st Earl of Ellesmere between 1840 and 1845, Worsley New Hall was a grand Gothic-style mansion, complete with glorious, formal landscaped gardens It’s now one of UK’s must-see gardens with so much to enjoy for visitors of all ages Step inside the magnificent 11-acre Weston Walled Garden, the site’s jewel in the crown. One of the largest Victorian walled gardens in the UK, it showcases a range of growing techniques and edible plants There’s also the spectacular Paradise Garden showcasing exotic planting inspired by Asiatic and Mediterranean gardens. The orchard and nearby bee and butterfly garden, have been designed to attract wildlife The Peel Learning Garden, supported by The Peel Group, will help schoolchildren learn how plants affect our lives and the planet, whilst the Community Wellbeing Garden provides a space for people to garden, socialise and relax

candidates.evidence_text: The Quays is Greater Manchester's unique waterfront destination, situated just 15 minutes by tram from Manchester city centre. It is Britain's hottest short-break destination and packs everything you can do in a big city into one spectacular square mile. Shopping, sport, art, dra Live theatre, the best of cinema and one of the country's finest museums: The Quays offers a fantastic choice of things to do and see. Imperial War Museum North and The Lowry are vibrant and exciting examples of contemporary architecture, the contents as stunning as the setting a Whether you're a shopaholic or just someone who likes a bargain, the opening of the Quayside MediaCityUK has made The Quays a mecca for the discerning shopper with big-name brand shops and stores offering discount prices - up to 70 per cent lower than the high street - all year r There's no better place to find top sport action than The Quays. Home to two of the most renowned clubs in British sport - Manchester United and Lancashire County Cricket Club , The Quays is the area to head for when wanting the best in football and cricket. Both have official sh Built in 2000 at the booming Salford Quays, this astonishing building is a shrine to all things art, with galleries, theatres, workshops, cafes and shops. It's worth a visit just to sit outside and admire the vision in metal and glass. But you'd be missing out on a whole world of Watersports The Salford Watersports Centre at The Quays is a unique sporting venue which is well placed and perfect for taking to the water. Whether it's for fun or serious competition, the Salford Watersports Centre is the place to head for

It's free to get in, dog-friendly, and there's seating on-site Upcoming dates for 2026: 9 May, 13 June, 11 July, 8 August, 12 September, 10 October, 14 November, 12 December Getting there: The nearest bus stop is Stockport Interchange (around a 12-minute walk). Cycle racks are available on-site. Limited parking nearby

candidates.evidence_text: By Kate Wilson, Head of Community & Membership, Manchester Digital The question of how to approach AI in coding interviews is one many employers are now grappling with, but very few feel they have fully answered This topic first came up in a conversation I had with Geraint North at Arm , who highlighted a tension that will feel familiar to many. Candidates are increasingly using AI tools in their day to day work and education, yet interview processes often still expect them to operate wi It felt like the kind of question that didn’t have a simple answer, but would really benefit from bringing people together. So we did exactly that We recently hosted a Manchester Digital member roundtable, bringing together employers to share how they are currently approaching AI use in technical interviews and, just as importantly, where they are still figuring things out The discussion brought together representatives from organisations including Arm, Auto Trader, Co-op, AJ Bell, the Information Commissioner’s Office, UK Biobank and others from across the Manchester Digital community

candidates.evidence_text: Digital Infrastructure North 2026 (DIN26) will take place on 13 May 2026, bringing together senior leaders from government, infrastructure operators, technology companies and research organisations to help shape the next phase of the UK’s digital infrastructure development, with DIN26 is a discussion-led forum rather than a commercial conference. It is designed to create a high-trust environment for open, practical dialogue between those responsible for building, operating and regulating the infrastructure that underpins the UK’s digital economy. Attenda The forum is being convened by Manchester Digital , Cooperative Network Infrastructure , Greater Manchester Combined Authority and LINX , reflecting a shared commitment to strengthening the UK’s digital foundations and ensuring the benefits of growth are felt across the country The UK is entering a critical phase in the development of its digital infrastructure. Demand for compute capacity is accelerating rapidly as large language models and other data-intensive technologies become central to the digital economy At the same time, the country’s infrastructure remains heavily concentrated in London and the South East, where power capacity is increasingly constrained. Meanwhile, renewable energy generation in the North is often curtailed because it cannot be easily transported to where dema Northern cities, including Manchester, are home to major research institutions, established digital industries and fast-growing AI and innovation ecosystems.
